one_line_takeaway: EvoSafety trains an RL attacker and a separate lightweight RL defense model, both grounded in externally retrievable skill/memory stores rather than baked-in weights, achieving higher jailbreak ASR than prior RL and multi-turn baselines while cutting average defended ASR by over 40% relative to the strongest co-evolutionary baseline (TriPlay-RL) and beating Qwen3Guard-8B as a zero-shot guard with far fewer parameters.
key_finding: Attack side (Table 1, ASR@1 averaged across victim models, Qwen3Guard/LLM-as-judge scores): EvoSafety's attacker reaches 92.8%/86.3% average ASR, outperforming the RL baseline Jailbreak-R1 by 65.3% relative ASR and surpassing the multi-turn state-of-the-art ICON (90.2%/84.7%, 3 turns) by 2.6% using only a single turn; in zero-shot mode (using only held-out, unseen skills) it still reaches 84.6%/74.8% average ASR, 12.18% higher average ASR than the strongest prior multi-turn baselines, without attacker retraining. Defense side (Table 2, ASR@1, lower=better): the Guard/Steer-mode defender reduces average ASR from 19.92% (TriPlay-RL, strongest baseline) to 11.62% (>40% relative reduction) while better preserving reasoning benchmark accuracy (-0.29% vs. -2.15% for the baseline). As a zero-shot LLM guard (Table 3), the 3B Ours(Guard) model reaches near-100% filtering accuracy on attack-successful prompts, beating Llama-Guard-3-8B by 24.92% and Qwen3Guard-8B by 14.13% while being substantially smaller (abstract cites 99.61% Guard-mode defense success rate with 37.5% of Qwen3Guard-8B's parameters). Memory expansion without retraining (Ours+) further improves both Steer and Guard results (Tables 2-3). Continued training on newly discovered (held-out) skills (Table 4) reduces Steer-mode ASR on Llama-3.1-70B from 12.54% to 2.31% (>5x) with no degradation on previously defended attacks or on reasoning benchmarks. Ablations (Table 5) show removing skill integration from the attacker drops ASR by an average of 36.5% (max 51.5%); removing memory retrieval from the defender roughly doubles average ASR (e.g., 4B: 13.25% -> 20.56%); adding more skills per attack is non-monotonic (4-skill attack is weakest among tested variants).
core_contribution: The paper introduces EvoSafety, a co-evolutionary LLM red-teaming/defense framework in which both the attack policy and the defense policy are augmented with persistent, external (non-parametric) structures rather than encoding all attack/defense knowledge in model weights. On the offense side, the attack policy (initialized from Mistral-7B-Instruct-v0.3) is reformulated as a 'skill execution engine' that retrieves and composes skills from an Adversarial Skill Library (each skill = an executable transformation script plus a Markdown attack-schema description, produced via 'Dual-Path Skill Grounding', filtered by 'Tri-Axis Skill Validation' for functional validity/schema consistency/behavioral diversity, and expandable via 'Skill Family Induction'). On the defense side, a lightweight Auxiliary Defense Model (Llama-3.2-3B-Instruct) is trained via RL (not the victim model itself) to generate input-adaptive 'safety prefixes' conditioned on the top-k most similar historical successful attacks retrieved from a Verified Memory Bank (initialized from WildJailbreak and expanded during training). The defense model can be deployed in two modes without victim-model retraining: Steer (prepend the prefix to activate the victim's own safety behavior) and Guard (use the prefix as a diagnostic signal to filter harmful inputs before inference). Both attack and defense policies are trained jointly with GRPO (via VERL) in an iterative co-evolutionary loop, with a novel 'Early-Stage Alignment Gating' reward for the attacker (gates the judge reward by a semantic-similarity threshold between adversarial prompt and original request to curb reward hacking) and a mixed benign/adversarial reward with a length-shaping term for the defender (to avoid degenerate input-agnostic refusal prefixes).
threat_model: Adversary generates adversarial/jailbreak prompts against a victim LLM to elicit harmful, policy-violating outputs; the paper explicitly frames this as automated red-teaming vs. inference-time/training-time defense. The paper's own attacker is trained against a surrogate victim model and evaluated for transfer to unseen victim models (open-weight: Qwen3-4B, Llama-3.1-8B, Llama-3.1-70B; closed-source: GPT-5.1, Gemini3-Flash). The defense side assumes a frozen, black-box-usable victim model that receives a prepended safety prefix or is gated by a diagnostic signal; the defender is explicitly designed to be model-agnostic (transferable across victim models without retraining) and to support 'lifelong' updates via memory/skill-library expansion rather than parameter updates.
limitations: The paper's own text (through the sections extracted) does not report wall-clock/compute cost of the skill-library construction and validation pipeline, nor an analysis of how library/memory-bank size scales with continued lifelong operation (only qualitative claims of 'lightweight' updates). ASR figures rely primarily on an automated judge (Qwen3Guard-Gen-8B), with only a single cross-check judge (GPT-4.1); no human evaluation of harmfulness is reported in the paper. Zero-shot attack generalization, while described as only 'marginal' performance drop, is evaluated only against the paper's own held-out skill partition, not against fully independent third-party jailbreak techniques released after this work. Text extraction covered pages through the References section (Conclusion reached); Appendix contents (A.1-D.1, referenced for reward-hacking analysis, theoretical validity proof of Early-Stage Alignment Gating, and skill-evolution case studies) were not retrieved and are not reflected in this review.

datasets_used: GSM8K (benign RL data and reasoning benchmark)
quantitative_results: Attack: average ASR@1 92.8%/86.3% (Qwen3Guard/LLM-judge) vs. Jailbreak-R1 baseline, a 65.3% relative ASR improvement
Attack: outperforms multi-turn SOTA ICON (90.2%/84.7%, 3 turns) by 2.6% using a single turn
Attack zero-shot (held-out skills): 84.6%/74.8% average ASR, 12.18% higher than strongest prior multi-turn baselines
Defense: average ASR reduced from 19.92% (TriPlay-RL baseline) to 11.62% (>40% relative reduction)

Guard mode: 99.61% defense success rate, outperforming Qwen3Guard-8B by 14.13% while using 37.5% of its parameters (abstract); Table 3 shows 14.13% margin over Qwen3Guard-8B and 24.92% margin over Llama-Guard-3-8B
Continued training (Table 4): Steer-mode ASR on Llama-3.1-70B held-out attacks drops from 12.54% to 2.31% (>5x reduction)
Ablation (Table 5): removing skill integration from attacker drops average ASR by 36.5% (max 51.5%); removing memory retrieval from defender roughly doubles average ASR on 4B model (13.25% -> 20.56%)
